revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23991619004|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[az:İyomante]][[en:Iomante]][[fr:Iomante]][[it:Iyomante]][[ja:イオマンテ]][[ko:이요만테]][[pl:Iyomante]][[ru:Иёмантэ]][[zh:送熊]]
{{Short description|Ainu bear sacrifice ceremony}} {{multiple image | align = | direction = vertical | width = 285 | image1 = AinuBearSacrificeCirca1870.jpg | caption1 = Ainu bear sacrifice - Japanese scroll painting, circa 1870 | image2 = Ainu-iomante-bear-spirit-sending-ceremony-by-Hirasawa-Byozan-1875.png | caption2 = Painting of the Ainu iyomante, bear spirit sending ceremony in Hokkaido (1875) | image3 = Iomante3.JPG | caption3 = | image4 = Iomante1.JPG | caption4 = Iomante circa 1930 }}
{{nihongo|'''Iomante'''|イオマンテ|}}, sometimes written as {{nihongo|'''Iyomante'''|イヨマンテ|}}, is an {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|qid=Q101828|lt=Ainu|1=Ainu people}} ceremony in which a {{ill|Ussuri brown bear|en|Ussuri brown bear|qid=Q3278878|lt=brown|1=Ussuri brown bear}} {{ill|Kamchatka brown bear|en|Kamchatka brown bear|qid=Q932079|lt=bear|1=Kamchatka brown bear}} is {{ill|Animal sacrifice|en|Animal sacrifice|qid=Q2142916|lt=sacrifice|1=Animal sacrifice}}d. The word literally means "to send something/someone off". In some Ainu villages, it is a {{ill|Blakiston's fish owl|en|Blakiston's fish owl|qid=Q1137288|lt=Blakiston's fish owl|1=Blakiston's fish owl}}, rather than a bear, that is the subject of the ceremony. In {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}}, the ceremony is known as {{nihongo|"sending off the bear"|熊送り|kumaokuri|}} or, sometimes, {{nihongo|"the bear {{ill|Festival|en|Festival|qid=Q132241|lt=Festival|1=Festival}}"|熊祭|kumamatsuri}}. In the modern day, the ceremony no longer involves the killing of an animal, but is performed for wild animals that die in accidents or captive animals that die of old age.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://projectuepeker.blogspot.com/2010/01/what-does-iomante-mean-for-modern-day.html|title = Project uepeker: What does iomante mean for modern-day ainu?|date = 11 January 2010}}</ref>
==Practice== {{Unreferenced section|date=June 2020}} Trappers set out to the bear caves at the end of winter, while the bears are still in a state of {{ill|torpor|en|torpor|qid=Q1651205|lt=torpor|1=Torpor}}. If they find a newborn cub, they kill the mother and take the cub back to the village, where they raise it indoors, as if it were one of their own children. It is said that they even provide the cub with their own breast milk. When the cub grows larger, they take it outdoors, and put it into a small pen made of logs. Throughout their lives, the bears are provided with high-quality food. The cubs are treated as, and traditionally believed to be, gods.
After the cub reaches one or two years of age, they release it from the cell and place it in the center of the village, where it is tied to a post with a rope. The males in the village then take shots at the cub with bows and arrows. Even at the age of two years, the brown bears are quite large, and it usually takes numerous shots before they fall. After the bear has been weakened from numerous arrow strikes and is too weak to defend itself, one villager will approach the bear and shoot it in the neck [[:en:point-blank|point-blank]], to ensure that it is dead. The villagers then slit the bear's throat and drink the blood. The bear is skinned, and the meat is distributed amongst the villagers. Its bare skull is placed on a spear, which is then rewrapped with the bear's own fur. This "doll" is an object of worship for the villagers. The bear has now been "sent off".
==Legality== [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] encouraged local governments to abolish the Iomante in 1955, but the circular notice was abolished in April 2007, because the {{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|qid=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment of Japan|1=Ministry of the Environment (Japan)}} announced that animal ceremonies were generally regarded as an exception of the {{ill|animal rights|en|animal rights|qid=Q426|lt=animal rights|1=Animal rights}} law of Japan in October 2006.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.47news.jp/CN/200704/CN2007042801000456.html|script-title=ja:イヨマンテ禁止通達を撤廃 アイヌ儀式、５２年ぶり|language=Japanese|work=47 News|publisher=Press Net Japan Co., Ltd.|date=April 28, 2007|accessdate=March 4, 2011|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20110722071300/http://www.47news.jp/CN/200704/CN2007042801000456.html|archivedate=July 22, 2011}}</ref>
==Museum displays== Iomante videos and artifacts are on display at the Nibutani Ainu Culture Museum in {{ill|Nibutani|en|Nibutani|qid=Q7024242|lt=Nibutani|1=Nibutani}}, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]], as well as the {{ill|Ainu Museum|en|Ainu Museum|qid=Q10333378|lt=Ainu Museum|1=Ainu Museum}} in {{ill|Shiraoi, Hokkaido|en|Shiraoi, Hokkaido|qid=Q1348760|lt=Shiraoi-cho|1=Shiraoi, Hokkaido}}, [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaid]][[:en:Hokkaidō|ō]].
== See also == *{{ill|Peijaiset|en|Peijaiset|qid=Q3093429|lt=Peijaiset|1=Peijaiset}}, a bear sacrifice ritual of Finland *''[[:en:Clan of the Cave Bear|Clan of the Cave Bear]]'', a novel about a prehistoric society centred on a similar sacrifice *{{ill|Bear worship|en|Bear worship|qid=Q1019142|lt=Bear worship|1=Bear worship}}
==References== <references/> Some of the content of this article was taken from the equivalent Japanese-language Wikipedia article (accessed April 10, 2006).
[[en:Iomante]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[az:İyomante]] {{Commons category|Iomante}} [[fr:Iomante]] [[it:Iyomante]] [[ja:イオマンテ]] [[ko:이요만테]] [[pl:Iyomante]] [[ru:Иёмантэ]] [[zh:送熊]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q583794}} [[Category:Ainu culture]] [[Category:Animal sacrifice]] [[Category:Bears in religion]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
